sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see: there are 2 yellow shapes (7 pixels each) in the bottom container, and the yellow bar has 23 pixels. The total is 14 + 23 = 37. If each click transfers 7 pixels (the shape size), then 23/7 ≈ 3.3, which doesn't divide evenly. But the green box had 14 pixels and each U-shape has 7 pixels, so 14/7 = 2 clicks would empty it.
Wait, but when I clicked the left green U-shape, the green box went from 14→5 (removed 9 pixels, not 7). And when I clicked the right green U-shape, it went from 5→14 (added 9 pixels).
Hmm, let me reconsider. The green box is 3x5 = 15 pixels minus the 1 pixel red dot = 14 pixels. After the first click it became 5 pixels. The difference is 9 pixels.
Actually wait - let me look more carefully. The initial green box boundary was [[9, 28], [9, 32], [11, 32], [11, 28]] which is a 3x4 rectangle = 12 pixels. But the pixel count was 14. That's odd. Let me re-examine.
Actually, the boundary is clockwise outer-perimeter corner points. [[9, 28], [9, 32], [11, 32], [11, 28]] means rows 9-11, cols 28-32. That's 3 rows × 5 cols = 15 cells. But 14 pixels because one cell is the red dot.
After click: boundary [[9, 28], [9, 29], [11, 29], [11, 28], [10, 29]] - this is a smaller shape with 5 pixels. The red dot moved from (10, 31) to (10, 28).
After second click: boundary [[9, 28], [9, 32], [11, 32], [11, 28]] - back to the original 3x5 rectangle = 15 cells minus 1 red dot = 14 pixels.
So the green box can be in two states: full (14 pixels) or shrunk (5 pixels). The U-shapes toggle between these states.
Let me try clicking on the yellow shapes to see what happens. I'll click on the top yellow shape first.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the top yellow shape in the bottom container
# Top yellow shape center is around row 38, col 24
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 38, 'col': 24}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
